skill.name: accessibility-auditor
skill.description: Reviews UI components for WCAG compliance, ARIA attributes, keyboard navigation, and screen reader support. Use when building frontend components or user requests accessibility improvements.
skill.summary: Audits frontend UI for WCAG 2.1 AA, ARIA, keyboard access, and screen reader support.
skill.author: CrazyDubya

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, weak cryptography, sensitive key, reconnaissance, and obfuscation patterns. Manual review found these are documentation examples, markdown syntax, WCAG checklist text, sample URLs, and keyboard event examples, with no confirmed malicious behavior or prompt injection.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Examples Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command alerts are caused by fenced markdown examples, HTML tags in backticks, JavaScript keyboard examples, and documented npx accessibility tool commands. The skill declares only Read, Grep, and Glob tools, so no shell execution capability is granted by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is documentation text and example snippets, not executable skill code. The allowed tools line confirms the skill cannot invoke shell commands directly.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto and Sensitive Key Alerts
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and certificate/key alerts map to accessibility terms, form examples, and keyboard event text such as password help, aria-describedby, page titles, and e.key. No cryptographic API, certificate file access, private key material, or credential handling is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual line review found accessibility checklist prose and UI examples only. There is no evidence of crypto implementation, certificate loading, or secret collection.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Network and Reconnaissance Heuristic
low_findings.description: The network and reconnaissance alerts are caused by sample URLs for Lighthouse, axe, and pa11y, plus checklist words such as screen readers and valid HTML. No fetch call, HTTP client, socket use, scanning loop, or external exfiltration destination was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only URLs are placeholders inside optional CLI examples. No operational network code or reconnaissance instruction is present in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Dangerous Combination Heuristic
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined documentation-only command examples, placeholder URLs, and accessibility form examples. No code execution, credential access, network exfiltration, obfuscation, or prompt injection attempt was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static heuristic has no semantic support after reviewing the referenced content. The skill is an accessibility checklist with read-only declared tools.

content.value_statement: Teams often miss WCAG, ARIA, keyboard, and screen reader issues during frontend development. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused accessibility review checklist for practical UI improvements.
actual_capabilities: Reviews UI markup for semantic HTML and proper landmark structure.
Checks ARIA roles, labels, descriptions, and live region usage.
Identifies keyboard navigation, tab order, focus management, and keyboard trap issues.
Assesses image alt text, form labels, error messages, and heading hierarchy.
Provides WCAG 2.1 Level AA checklist guidance for manual and automated testing.
Suggests accessibility testing tools such as Lighthouse, axe, pa11y, and screen readers.
limitations: It cannot certify legal WCAG compliance without human testing and assistive technology review.
It does not run automated scanners unless the user provides an execution environment and permission.
It may miss issues that require live interaction, user research, or production analytics.
It focuses on WCAG 2.1 Level AA and may not cover every WCAG 2.2 or regional requirement.

use_cases.title: Review a New Component
use_cases.description: Find semantic HTML, ARIA, keyboard, form, and screen reader issues before a component ships.

use_cases.title: Prepare for an Accessibility Audit
use_cases.description: Use a WCAG 2.1 AA checklist to organize issues and reduce audit findings.

use_cases.title: Improve Design Handoff Quality
use_cases.description: Check interaction states, labels, contrast expectations, and keyboard behavior before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Component Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one UI component.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this component for accessibility issues. Focus on semantic HTML, labels, alt text, and keyboard access.

prompt_templates.title: WCAG Checklist Pass
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this page against WCAG 2.1 Level AA. Group findings by perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust.

prompt_templates.title: Interactive Flow Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of modals, menus, tabs, or forms.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this interaction flow for focus management, tab order, ARIA state changes, error messages, and screen reader announcements.

prompt_templates.title: Release Readiness Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized accessibility release review. Include likely user impact, WCAG mapping, manual tests, and automated checks to run.

output_examples.input: Review a modal dialog before release.
output: The dialog needs an accessible name and initial focus on the first meaningful control.
Trap focus while the dialog is open and return focus to the trigger after close.
Confirm Escape closes the dialog and that screen readers announce validation errors.

output_examples.input: Check a product card grid for WCAG issues.
output: Each product image needs useful alt text or an empty alt attribute when decorative.
Button and link labels should describe the target product, not only say View or Buy.
Verify color contrast for prices, sale labels, and disabled states.

output_examples.input: Prepare accessibility tests for a form.
output: Confirm every input has a visible label or accessible name.
Associate help text and errors with the relevant fields.
Test keyboard submission, validation announcements, and zoom at 200 percent.

best_practices: Provide the component, page context, and expected user interaction path.
Ask for findings grouped by severity, WCAG criterion, and affected user impact.
Combine automated checks with keyboard and screen reader manual testing.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on ARIA when native semantic HTML can solve the issue.
Do not treat a passing automated scan as complete accessibility compliance.
Do not ignore focus order, visible focus states, or keyboard-only workflows.

faq.question: Does this skill run accessibility scanners automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and tool suggestions. Scanner execution requires user permission and an available environment.

faq.question: Which accessibility standard does it target?
faq.answer: It focuses on WCAG 2.1 Level AA, with practical checks for common frontend accessibility issues.

faq.question: Can it replace a formal accessibility audit?
faq.answer: No. It helps find and organize issues, but formal audits need human testing and assistive technology review.

faq.question: Can it review React or other component frameworks?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review rendered markup patterns, component code, ARIA usage, focus behavior, and form handling.

faq.question: Does it cover screen reader testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains what to check, including labels, announcements, live regions, and reader-only text.

faq.question: Is it suitable for design reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review labels, contrast expectations, interaction states, keyboard behavior, and accessibility acceptance criteria.
